topic: A person accidentally sends a text to the wrong recipient
raw_output:
[story 1]

My thumb hovered over the screen, a nervous tremor running through my hand. I was trying to send a quick update to Sarah about the project deadline, but my eyes snagged on the wrong contact name—a number I vaguely recognized from a group chat, but definitely not Sarah. A wave of sickening dread washed over me. With a clumsy swipe, the message shot out. A second later, a notification chimed, and I saw the reply pop up from a stranger. My breath hitched. I stared at the screen, the blood draining from my face. Every muscle in my body tightened; I felt suddenly small and exposed. I immediately started frantically typing an apology, my fingers clumsy and shaking as I tried to backtrack, praying the damage was minimal. The silence in my small apartment felt deafening, amplifying the frantic thumping in my chest.

[story 2]

The anxiety was a tight band around my ribs. I was supposed to be confirming the reservation details with Mark, but my focus had completely fractured. My phone buzzed, and instead of the familiar green bubble, it was a notification from an unknown number. My heart leaped into my throat. I glanced at the text, and a cold sweat broke out across my forehead. It was clearly addressed to someone else entirely. A profound sense of panic seized me, making my limbs feel heavy and unresponsive. I wanted to shove the phone away, to disappear, but I couldn't move. I just sat there, staring at the glowing screen, every nerve ending screaming. I kept re-reading the message, my eyes darting back and forth, searching for some clue, some reason why this had happened.

[story 3]

I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight. I had intended to send a casual greeting to my mentor, a simple check-in. But my hand slipped, and the wrong contact illuminated the screen. The realization hit me like a physical blow, stealing the air from my lungs. A dizzying feeling washed over me, making the room spin slightly. My jaw went slack. I felt an overwhelming urge to apologize, to fix this catastrophic error instantly. My fingers hovered over the keyboard, paralyzed by the magnitude of the mistake. The silence stretched out, thick and suffocating, as I waited for the inevitable response, every internal monologue a frantic, repetitive loop of 'what have I done?'
